fr:title.text: Associativity and unit laws of matrix products ›

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Associativity and unit laws of matrix products ›

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For associativity, fix matrices L\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (k,l\right )\mathclose {}}, M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (l,m\right )\mathclose {}} and N\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} to check that {\mathopen {}\left (N\otimes M\right )\mathclose {}}\otimes L = N\otimes {\mathopen {}\left (M\otimes L\right )\mathclose {}}. Below we use the associativity of multiplication, commutativity of addition, and distributivity of multiplication over addition in the semiring S:
For one unit law, we fix M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} and recall the definition of the identity matrix to check that M\otimes I^m = M.

Unfolding the definition of I^m_{i,j} as given in Definition , we see that the iterated sum above expands to m-1 copies of 0\odot M_{k,j} for various k \not = i and one copy of 1\odot M_{i,j}. Thus, we conclude that M\otimes I^m_{i,j} = M_{i,j} using the absorption and unit laws for multiplication as well as the unit laws for addition in S.
The other unit law follows in an analogous way.

fr:title.text: Associativity and unit laws of matrix products

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Associativity and unit laws of matrix products

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The product of matrices is associative and has the identity matrix as neutral element.

For associativity, fix matrices L\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (k,l\right )\mathclose {}}, M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (l,m\right )\mathclose {}} and N\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} to check that {\mathopen {}\left (N\otimes M\right )\mathclose {}}\otimes L = N\otimes {\mathopen {}\left (M\otimes L\right )\mathclose {}}. Below we use the associativity of multiplication, commutativity of addition, and distributivity of multiplication over addition in the semiring S:
For one unit law, we fix M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} and recall the definition of the identity matrix to check that M\otimes I^m = M.

Unfolding the definition of I^m_{i,j} as given in Definition , we see that the iterated sum above expands to m-1 copies of 0\odot M_{k,j} for various k \not = i and one copy of 1\odot M_{i,j}. Thus, we conclude that M\otimes I^m_{i,j} = M_{i,j} using the absorption and unit laws for multiplication as well as the unit laws for addition in S.
The other unit law follows in an analogous way.

For natural numbers m and n, an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring {\mathopen {}\left (S,0,\oplus ,1,\odot \right )\mathclose {}} is given by entries M_{i,j}\in S for all i\in {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} and j\in {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}. We will write \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} for the set of {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrices.
For any m\in \mathbb {N}, we define the identity {\mathopen {}\left (m\times m\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a given semiring S as follows:
The identity matrix is sometimes called the diagonal matrix, for reasons that become apparent when visualising it according to Notation :
